109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}

117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}

123	\end{theorem}

125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}

135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.

154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}

219	  \mathlibok

210	\begin{lemma}
211	  [Direct section-level value of the affine base-change map]
212	  \label{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_sections_direct}

217	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
218	  In the affine-affine model --- S = Spec R, S' = Spec R', X = Spec A,
219	  F = M̃ for an A-module M, g = Spec ψ (ψ : R → R'), f = Spec φ (φ : R → A) ---
220	  the composition
221	  \[
222	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
223	      \;\circ\; \Gamma\!\bigl(\alpha\bigr)
224	      \;\circ\; \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
225	    \;:\; R' \otimes_R M
226	    \;\longrightarrow\; (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
227	  \]
228	  equals the inverse cancellation isomorphism
229	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1} : r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\)
230	  of Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}, where
231	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are the domain and codomain
232	  identifications of Lemmas~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}
233	  and~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.

239	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
240	  The proof is a direct module computation. The map
241	  \(\alpha = \mathtt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) is defined as the natural
242	  transformation obtained from the adjunction
243	  \((f'^*, f'_*)\) and \((g^*, g_*)\) applied to the identity on \(f_* \widetilde M\).
244	  Concretely, on an affine chart the adjunction units/counits reduce to algebra maps
245	  between tensor products. Tracing \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) through its definition yields,
246	  on a generator \(r' \otimes m\):
247	  \begin{enumerate}
248	    \item Apply \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\): pass to the \(\Gamma\)-representation
249	      of the domain via the two tilde dictionaries
250	      (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}).
251	    \item Apply \(\Gamma(\alpha)\): use the explicit definition of

2468	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2469	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open

2497	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2498	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2499	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2500	  %  associated to $M$.
2501	  %  [...]

2504	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2505	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2506	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2507	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2508	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2509	  \begin{enumerate}
2510	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2511	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2512	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2513	    \item the section-restriction map
2514	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2515	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2516	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2517	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2518	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2519	  \end{enumerate}
2520	\end{lemma}

2522	\begin{proof}
2523	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2524	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2525	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2526	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2527	
2528	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2529	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2530	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2531	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2532	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2533	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2534	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2535	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2536	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2537	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2538	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2539	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2540	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2541	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2542	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2543	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2544	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2545	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2546	\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).

  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
  this identification across the affine isomorphism
  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}
  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).

  Part (2) is the \emph{gap-2 transport}: it reduces to G1-core
  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}) on the single affine chart
  \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Since \(U\) is affine, Mathlib's canonical isomorphism
  \(\varphi : U \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) identifies \(U\) with the
  spectrum of its ring of sections. Push \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) forward along
  \(\varphi\) to a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}'\) on \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\);
  because \(\mathcal{M}\) is quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence is preserved by
  pullback/pushforward along an isomorphism, \(\mathcal{M}'\) is quasi-coherent.
  Under \(\varphi\) the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\) corresponds to the standard
  basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) (the structure-sheaf section
  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\) transports to \(f \in \Gamma(\Spec\Gamma(X,U), \top)\)),
  and the section restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) corresponds,
  under the iso on sections, to the restriction
  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}', \top) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{M}', D(f))\).

  Now apply G1-core (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}) to the
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}'\) on the affine \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) and the
  element \(f\): the restriction
  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}', \top) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{M}', D(f))\) is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(\Gamma(X, U)\).
  Transporting this localization property back across the iso on sections induced
  by \(\varphi\) (using the standard transfer of \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\)
  along the pre/post-composing linear equivalences) shows the restriction
  \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is itself a localization at
  \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\).

  \emph{Remaining ingredient.} The chapter does not yet package the transport of
  the \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) predicate across the affine identification
  \(\varphi : U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) (carrying \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) to the
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}'\) and \(D(f)\) to the standard \(D(f)\)) as a named
  lemma; there is no Mathlib lemma performing this specific transport. This is the
  sole genuinely new piece the prover must supply on top of G1-core --- a
  \emph{single-chart} affine-isomorphism transport, \emph{not} a cover-and-glue
  over a family of affine opens (\(U\) is already affine, so no second cover of
  \(X\) is introduced and no ``infinite onion'' of nested gluings arises). It is
  local on \(U\) in the trivial sense that there is one chart.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullback_composite_immersion_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:composite_immersion_range_basicOpen, lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen,
    lem:gamma_image_iso_semilinear_top,
    lem:flocus_section_scalar_tower, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso, lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso}
  Form the composite open immersion \(j : \Spec R_s \to \Spec R\) and the pullback module
  \(M' := (\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M\). By
  \cref{lem:pullback_composite_immersion_isIso_fromTildeΓ},
  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,M'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\); hence by the affine engine
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} the section restriction
  \(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f')\) over
  \(R_s\), where \(f' = \sigma^{-1}(\mathrm{algebraMap}\,R\,R_s\,f)\) and
  \(j\,''^{U}\,D(f') = D(f) \sqcap D(s)\) (\cref{lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen}).

  \emph{Scalar-base reconciliation (no transport needed).} The affine engine produces its localization
  over the section ring \(R_s = \Gamma(q.X_i, W)\) viewed as a plain ring, whereas the semilinearity
  inputs below are stated over the structure-sheaf ring \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R_s}, \top)\). These
  two module structures on \(\Gamma(M', \top)\) coincide \emph{definitionally}: the tilde functor rebases
  the structure-sheaf action to the constant ring \(R_s\) by restriction of scalars along the global
  sections iso \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R_s}, \top) \cong R_s\), so the chosen
  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\) is exactly the
  inverse of that baked-in rebasing and no explicit scalar-restriction transport is required.

  Package the section transports as additive isomorphisms: the \(\top\)-level comparison
  \cref{lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso} gives \(e_1 : \Gamma(M', \top) \simeq_+ \Gamma(M, D(s))\), and the
  image-level comparison \cref{lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso} gives
  \(e_2 : \Gamma(M', D(f')) \simeq_+ \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\); both are \(\sigma\)-semilinear over
  \(\sigma = \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\) by \cref{lem:gamma_image_iso_semilinear_top}. The
  target rings carry the \(A = \Gamma(M, D(s))\)-module and \(R\)-module structures with scalar tower of
  \cref{lem:flocus_section_scalar_tower}, and the section restriction
  \(h : \Gamma(M, D(s)) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\) is \(A\)-linear and intertwines \(e_1, e_2\)
  with the \(R_s\)-localization \(g\). Feeding \(\sigma\), \(f\), \(f'\) (with
  \(\sigma(f') = \mathrm{algebraMap}\,R\,R_s\,f\), \cref{lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen}),
  \(g\), the semilinear pair \((e_1, e_2)\) and \(h\) (the \(e_2\) leg's semilinearity over the
  \(\top\)-level \(\sigma\) obtained from the restriction-square naturality
  \cref{lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_naturality}, not by re-stating \(\sigma\) at \(D(f')\)) to the
  combiner
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport} reads the localization back as
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\) on
  \(\Gamma(M, D(s)) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\), which is exactly the basic-open \(\mathrm{Hfr}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux, lem:pullback_composite_immersion_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:composite_immersion_range_basicOpen, lem:image_basicOpen_eq_inf,
    lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen, def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
  This is the thin instantiation of the opaque-\(j\) core
  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux} at the concrete composite immersion
  \(j = \) the immersion of \cref{def:composite_basic_open_immersion} identifying \(\Spec R_s\) with
  \(D(s)\). We discharge the four hypotheses of the core as follows.
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{P1 datum.} \cref{lem:pullback_composite_immersion_isIso_fromTildeΓ} supplies
      \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\).
    \item \emph{The element \(f'\).} Take
      \(f' := \sigma^{-1}(\mathrm{algebraMap}\,R\,R_s\,f)\) with
      \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\)
      (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}); then the \(\mathrm{appIso}\)-transport hypothesis is exactly
      \(\sigma(f') = \mathrm{algebraMap}\,R\,R_s\,f\), which holds by \(\sigma.\mathrm{apply\_symm\_apply}\)
      (claim (a) of \cref{lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen}).
    \item \emph{Identification of the opens.} \(j\,''^{U}\,\top = D(s)\) by
      \cref{lem:composite_immersion_range_basicOpen}; and
      \(j\,''^{U}\,D(f') = D(f) \sqcap D(s)\) by \cref{lem:image_basicOpen_eq_inf} (claim (b) of
      \cref{lem:composite_immersion_flocus_basicOpen}).
  \end{itemize}
  Feeding these to \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux} yields that the section restriction
  \(\Gamma(M, D(s)) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(s))\) is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\), which is the basic-open
  \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) datum. (The heavy assembly --- the \(\sigma\)-semilinear section transports, the
  scalar-tower instances, and the localization combiner --- is carried entirely by the core, which is
  why the immersion is kept opaque there; see \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux}.)
\end{proof}
